title: Multiple Quantum Hypothesis Testing: One-Shot Pairwise Bounds and Sharp Asymptotics
abstract: We consider Bayesian discrimination among multiple quantum states and establish a dimension-free one-shot upper bound on the minimum probability of error in terms of the sum of pairwise errors. This resolves a conjecture of Audenaert and Mosonyi [J. Math. Phys. 55 (2014)] and improves the multiple quantum Chernoff bound of Li [Ann. Statist. 44 (2016)] by removing its dimension-dependent prefactor. In the asymptotic many-copy regime, our bound proves the achievability of the multiple quantum Chernoff distance for arbitrary separable Hilbert spaces, thereby settling the previously open infinite-dimensional case, and further yields constant-factor sharp asymptotics for the optimal error probability.   In binary quantum hypothesis testing, we prove that the minimum error probability is characterized, up to universal constants, by a trace harmonic-mean quantity. Consequently, the optimal binary quantum error probability is within a factor of two of the optimal classical error probability for the associated Nussbaum-Szkoła distributions, complementing the lower bound of Nussbaum and Szkoła [Ann. Statist. 37 (2009)].
summary: This paper rigorously analyzes the minimum error probability when distinguishing between multiple unknown quantum states. It establishes sharp bounds by connecting the problem to operator means (like the harmonic mean) and proves that these bounds are achievable asymptotically. The results settle long-standing conjectures regarding the performance limits of quantum hypothesis testing in infinite dimensions.
main_problem: The core problem is establishing rigorous, sharp error bounds for discriminating among multiple quantum states ($\{ho_i\}$) in both finite-shot and asymptotic (many-copy) regimes.
main_result: The authors establish a dimension-free one-shot upper bound on the minimum probability of error and prove that this bound is achievable asymptotically, settling open problems regarding the multiple quantum Chernoff distance in infinite dimensions.
method: The analysis employs advanced tools from quantum information theory, including deriving bounds using operator means (like the harmonic mean), applying large deviation theory (Cramér's condition), and utilizing techniques like spectral decomposition and local limit theorems.
model_or_system: The system is general quantum hypothesis testing involving density operators $ho_i$ on a separable Hilbert space. The test uses an optimal Positive Operator-Valued Measure (POVM) $\{\Lambda_i\}$ to minimize the error probability.
key_observables: Minimum probability of decision error ($	ext{Err}^\star$), Quantum Chernoff distance ($C(ho_1, \dots, ho_M)$), and harmonic mean quantities ($	ext{HMs}(A, B)$).
important_parameters: Number of hypotheses ($M$), prior probabilities ($p_i$), number of copies ($n$), and the asymptotic parameter $s$ in operator means.
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes conditions required for large deviation theorems (e.g., non-orthogonality, finite moments) or relies on extending results from finite-rank approximations to general trace-class operators.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the setup of multiple quantum hypothesis testing using a POVM $\{\Lambda_i\}_{i=1}^M$. Equations detail asymptotic decay rates and harmonic mean relationships.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing general one-shot bounds for multiple hypotheses, to deriving sharp characterizations for the binary case using harmonic means, and finally extending these results to derive almost-exact asymptotics in the i.i.d. limit.
why_interesting: This work provides fundamental theoretical tools—sharp error exponents and generalized divergence measures—that govern the limits of quantum measurement capabilities, which is crucial for understanding resource theories in quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
